title: ACLS Cardiac Arrest Rhythms
objectives: Identify and differentiate the 4 cardiac arrest rhythms
Apply the correct ACLS algorithm for each rhythm
Understand the role of defibrillation vs medications

sections.title: The Four Cardiac Arrest Rhythms
sections.content:
Cardiac arrest rhythms are categorized into two groups based on treatment approach:

**Shockable Rhythms:**
• Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)
• Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (pVT)

**Non-Shockable Rhythms:**
• Asystole
• Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA)

This distinction drives the ACLS algorithm—shockable rhythms require immediate defibrillation, while non-shockable rhythms focus on CPR and reversible cause identification.

sections.title: Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)
sections.content:
**ECG Characteristics:**
• Chaotic, irregular waveforms
• No discernible P waves, QRS complexes, or T waves
• Variable amplitude (coarse vs fine VF)
• Rate: Cannot be determined

**Mechanism:**
Multiple reentrant circuits cause disorganized ventricular depolarization. The heart quivers rather than contracts—no cardiac output.

**Treatment Priority:**
1. Immediate high-quality CPR
2. Defibrillation as soon as available (200J biphasic)
3. Resume CPR immediately after shock
4. Epinephrine 1mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes
5. Amiodarone 300mg IV/IO (first dose), then 150mg

**Key Point:** Defibrillation is the ONLY definitive treatment. Every minute without defibrillation decreases survival by 7-10%.

sections.title: Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (pVT)
sections.content:
**ECG Characteristics:**
• Wide QRS complexes (>0.12 sec)
• Regular rhythm, rate typically 150-250 bpm
• No pulse despite organized electrical activity
• AV dissociation may be present

**Mechanism:**
A single reentrant circuit or automatic focus in the ventricle fires rapidly. Unlike VF, there's organized electrical activity, but mechanical function is inadequate.

**Treatment:**
Identical to VF—this is a SHOCKABLE rhythm:
1. Immediate defibrillation
2. CPR between shocks
3. Epinephrine and amiodarone per algorithm

**Clinical Pearl:** VT with a pulse is treated differently (synchronized cardioversion, antiarrhythmics). Always check for a pulse!

sections.title: Asystole
sections.content:
**ECG Characteristics:**
• Flat line or near-flat line
• No P waves, no QRS complexes
• May have very low amplitude undulations

**Critical First Step:**
Confirm in multiple leads! Apparent asystole may be fine VF in a single lead.

**Mechanism:**
Complete absence of electrical activity. Often represents end-stage of prolonged VF or severe metabolic derangement.

**Treatment (Non-Shockable):**
1. High-quality CPR
2. Epinephrine 1mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes
3. Search aggressively for reversible causes
4. Consider termination if no ROSC and no reversible cause

**Prognosis:**
Worst prognosis of all arrest rhythms. Survival <5% for out-of-hospital asystole.

**Do NOT shock asystole**—it won't help and delays CPR.

sections.title: Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA)
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Organized electrical activity on monitor WITHOUT a palpable pulse.

**ECG Characteristics:**
• Any organized rhythm (sinus, junctional, ventricular)
• May look completely normal
• KEY: No pulse despite ECG activity

**Mechanism:**
Electrical-mechanical dissociation. The heart's electrical system works, but mechanical contraction is absent or insufficient. This is NOT a rhythm problem—it's a pump or circulation problem.

**The H's and T's (Reversible Causes):**

**H's:**
• Hypovolemia (most common)
• Hypoxia
• Hydrogen ion (acidosis)
• Hypo/Hyperkalemia
• Hypothermia

**T's:**
• Tension pneumothorax
• Tamponade (cardiac)
• Toxins
• Thrombosis (pulmonary—massive PE)
• Thrombosis (coronary—MI)

**Treatment:**
1. CPR + Epinephrine (non-shockable)
2. Identify and treat reversible cause
3. Consider bedside ultrasound to assess cardiac activity

sections.title: The ACLS Cardiac Arrest Algorithm
sections.content:
**Universal Steps (All Rhythms):**
1. Start CPR immediately (push hard, push fast, minimize interruptions)
2. Give oxygen, attach monitor/defibrillator
3. Check rhythm—is it shockable?

**Shockable Pathway (VF/pVT):**
• Shock → CPR 2 min → Check rhythm
• Epinephrine after 2nd shock, then every 3-5 min
• Amiodarone after 3rd shock
• Continue cycle: Shock → CPR → Drug → Check rhythm

**Non-Shockable Pathway (Asystole/PEA):**
• CPR 2 min → Check rhythm
• Epinephrine immediately, then every 3-5 min
• Treat reversible causes aggressively
• If rhythm becomes shockable → switch pathways

**CPR Quality Targets:**
• Rate: 100-120 compressions/min
• Depth: At least 2 inches (5 cm)
• Full chest recoil
• Minimize interruptions (<10 sec for rhythm checks)

sections.title: Medications in Cardiac Arrest
sections.content:
**Epinephrine:**
• Dose: 1mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes
• Mechanism: Alpha-1 vasoconstriction increases coronary perfusion pressure
• Used in ALL cardiac arrest rhythms
• Timing: After 2nd shock in VF/pVT; immediately in asystole/PEA

**Amiodarone:**
• First dose: 300mg IV/IO bolus
• Second dose: 150mg IV/IO
• Used ONLY in refractory VF/pVT (after 3rd shock)
• Alternative: Lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg

**Vasopressin:**
• May replace first or second epinephrine dose
• Dose: 40 units IV/IO (single dose)
• No longer preferred in current guidelines

**Sodium Bicarbonate:**
• NOT routine
• Consider in: known hyperkalemia, TCA overdose, prolonged arrest
• Dose: 1 mEq/kg IV

sections.title: Post-Cardiac Arrest Care
sections.content:
After ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation):

**Immediate Priorities:**
1. Optimize oxygenation (SpO2 92-98%, avoid hyperoxia)
2. Maintain BP (MAP >65, may need vasopressors)
3. 12-lead ECG—emergent cath if STEMI
4. Avoid hyperthermia (target 32-36°C if comatose)

**Targeted Temperature Management (TTM):**
• For comatose survivors of cardiac arrest
• Target: 32-36°C for at least 24 hours
• Improves neurological outcomes

**Neurological Prognostication:**
• Wait at least 72 hours after normothermia
• Use multiple modalities (clinical exam, EEG, imaging, biomarkers)
• No single test is definitive

keyTakeaways: VF and pVT are shockable—defibrillate immediately
Asystole and PEA are non-shockable—focus on CPR and reversible causes
High-quality CPR is the foundation of all resuscitation
Epinephrine is used in all arrest rhythms; amiodarone only in refractory VF/pVT
Always search for H's and T's, especially in PEA
Post-arrest care focuses on oxygenation, hemodynamics, and temperature management

reviewQuestions.question: A patient is found unresponsive. The monitor shows a chaotic, irregular rhythm with no discernible QRS complexes. What is the immediate priority?
reviewQuestions.explanation: This is ventricular fibrillation—a shockable rhythm. Immediate defibrillation is the priority. Epinephrine comes after the 2nd shock, and amiodarone after the 3rd shock.

reviewQuestions.question: During a code, the monitor shows a regular wide-complex rhythm at 180 bpm. You check and there is no pulse. What type of rhythm is this?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Wide-complex, regular rhythm without a pulse = pulseless VT. This is a shockable rhythm treated the same as VF.

reviewQuestions.question: A trauma patient has a narrow-complex rhythm on the monitor but no pulse. What is the most likely reversible cause to consider first?
reviewQuestions.explanation: In trauma with PEA, hypovolemia from hemorrhage is the most common reversible cause. Other considerations include tension pneumothorax and cardiac tamponade.
